demos.evidence_text: Event pattern: Every 8 days on average.
demos.note_text:
Epilepsy Centre 

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 08 March 2016 

Dr Amelia Grant  
St. Cuthbert’s NHS Neurology Clinic  
Neurosciences Department, St. Cuthbert’s Hospital, Old Mill Road, York YO1 7AB  

Dear Dr Amelia Grant,  

Mr Daniel Reeves, DOB: 14-02-1990, Hospital No: YH4829301 NHS No. 9081764523  
Flat 3, 12 Wharf Lane, York YO10 3DT  

Diagnoses: 

Focal epilepsy. 

I reviewed Mr Daniel Reeves in clinic today. He works as a stage lighting technician with frequent exposure to strobe and bright lighting. He reports that colleagues may occasionally witness events during rig checks and rehearsals. He describes brief focal aware episodes with a rising epigastric sensation, a momentary pause, and lip smacking; awareness is usually preserved and he can often resume tasks within minutes. There is no generalisation reported. 

Event pattern: Every 8 days on average. He and his manager have been keeping a log, which aligns with this frequency across show weeks and dark periods. There is no clear clustering, but he notes that high-intensity strobe programming sessions and sleep deprivation may precede events. No tongue biting, incontinence, or prolonged post-ictal confusion has been reported. 

Medication and safety: No recent medication changes. He has not required emergency treatment and has not attended A&E. He uses agreed workplace strategies (colleague check-ins, stepping away from strobe programming when prodromal symptoms occur) and reports that these have reduced workplace disruption. 

Examination today was unremarkable. We discussed seizure first-aid with emphasis on maintaining a safe environment around lighting rigs. He prefers to continue current management while we complete an updated occupational risk assessment in collaboration with his employer. We will also liaise with his EEG appointment to include photic stimulation protocol, given the reported light exposure at work. 

Plan:  
- Continue current antiseizure therapy and seizure diary.  
- Workplace liaison letter to support scheduled breaks from intense strobe testing.  
- Routine EEG with photic stimulation and consider sleep-deprived EEG if diary suggests sleep triggers.  
- Review in three months or sooner if frequency increases or events evolve. 

Yours sincerely
demos.reasoning: The note states the event pattern is "Every 8 days on average," which corresponds to approximately 1 event per 8 days. This fits the format "1 per 8 day."

demos.evidence_text: Drop attacks are now reported several times weekly
demos.note_text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Fiona
Centre for Epilepsy
Queen Square, London WC1N 3BG

Dear Dr Fiona,

Susan Miller, DOB: 14-05-1986, Hospital No: P948572 NHS No. 7623549812
12 Baker Street, London, NW1 6XE

Epilepsy

Medication: Lamotrigine restarted in 2018 and continued since. Current regimen confirmed as lamotrigine dispersible 25 mg twice daily.

Lamotrigine Blood Level:
- Most recent check (patient reports September 2025 at local surgery): result not available to view today; last documented result on file remains <1 mg/L (reference 1.0–15.0 mg/L) when taking 25 mg twice daily. We have requested an updated level from the practice.

Seizures:
Since our last routine contact, there has been a notable deterioration associated with the current exam/professional assessment period and heightened stress, which the patient reports has been difficult to manage. Drop attacks are now reported several times weekly, having previously occurred only sporadically, once every few months. There are no consistent preceding auras or warnings described. She notes sudden loss of postural control with brief unresponsiveness on occasion, followed by rapid recovery, and transient confusion in a minority of events. No clear injury to date, though there have been near-falls; she is now avoiding stairs when alone. No nocturnal events reported by household members. Triggers described include sleep restriction and high-stakes revision sessions. Alcohol intake remains minimal; caffeine use has increased modestly during revision.

History Since Last Review:
- Patient-reported adherence to lamotrigine 25 mg twice daily without missed doses, using a phone reminder app.
- Increased stress due to upcoming professional examinations over the next six weeks.
- Sleep curtailed to approximately 5–6 hours on weeknights.
- No intercurrent illness, head injury, or medication changes otherwise. No over-the-counter remedies other than occasional paracetamol.

Plan Discussed Today (patient-reported preferences and information requests):
- The patient has asked for written information on stress management and sleep hygiene specific to seizure risk. She will trial structured breaks during revision and regular sleep–wake times.
- She has agreed to maintain a detailed seizure and trigger diary and to record any injuries or clusters.
- We have requested the GP surgery to repeat lamotrigine trough level, U&Es, LFTs, and a serum HCG as per our local protocol.
- Safety advice reinforced verbally: caution with bathing unsupervised, cooking at height, and use of ladders/stepladders; patient confirms understanding and will share advice with her household.
- We will bring forward her routine follow-up to a rapid-access review within 2–3 weeks by phone to reassess frequency and the laboratory results already requested from primary care.

Yours sincerely,
demos.reasoning: The note states that drop attacks are now reported several times weekly, having previously occurred sporadically once every few months. "Several times weekly" indicates multiple events per week. There is no more precise frequency given, so the best label is "multiple per week."

demos.evidence_text: He suffers clusters of absence seizures on five days each month.
demos.note_text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 February 2020 

Dr Susan  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, Queen Square, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Susan,  

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810  
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT 

Summary/Diagnoses: Focal epilepsy following prior traumatic brain injury; persistent cognitive impairment; osteoporosis; past alcohol dependence with alcoholic hepatitis; currently residing in supported accommodation; smoker; reports social alcohol use and is actively reducing intake. 

Medication: 
- Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily 
- Tegretol Retard (carbamazepine) 400 mg twice daily 
- Topiramate 75 mg morning and 100 mg evening 
- Vitamin B compound, Calcichew, Strontium sachets, Thiamine, Atorvastatin, Lansoprazole, Bisoprolol, Ramipril, Carbocisteine, Aqueous cream 

I reviewed Bill in clinic today, accompanied by his key worker, who has supported him for the past year. He reports improved sleep routine and better adherence to morning medications with use of a blister pack. He is engaging in light physiotherapy for balance and right-sided stiffness; gait is slow but he mobilises independently with a single-point stick. There have been no recent falls. Appetite is stable and weight is unchanged. He acknowledges occasional social alcohol use at family events but states he is reducing and aiming for abstinence; no binges have been reported by staff. 

Epilepsy history and current status: Bill has focal epilepsy. Seizure pattern has remained largely unchanged since the last clinic visit. He suffers clusters of absence seizures on five days each month. Nocturnal tonic seizures continue to occur around once per year. No daytime convulsions have been recorded recently. He and staff are keeping a simple monthly seizure diary, which aligns with the above pattern. Known triggers include missed morning doses and sleep loss; neither has occurred in the past two months according to care records. No injuries from seizures have been reported. 

Examination: Alert and oriented to person and place; mild executive dysfunction on conversational testing. Speech fluent. Cranial nerves intact. Tone mildly increased in right leg with reduced right ankle dorsiflexion; power otherwise antigravity throughout. Sensation grossly intact to light touch. Gait slow with reduced right foot clearance; no ataxia. Cardiovascular and respiratory exams unremarkable. 

Investigations: No new imaging since prior MRI showing left frontal gliosis consistent with old traumatic change. Routine bloods from GP two months ago reportedly normal including LFTs, U&Es, and vitamin D; we will request results for our records. No adverse haematological effects reported on current antiseizure regimen. 

Plan: 
- Continue current antiseizure medications unchanged given stable focal epilepsy pattern. 
- Reinforce adherence and sleep hygiene; continue seizure diary. 
- Request recent blood results from practice (FBC, U&E, LFT, vitamin D); repeat locally if unavailable. 
- Physiotherapy to continue for gait and right leg stiffness; encourage daily walking within supported setting. 
- Support ongoing reduction in social alcohol use; no medication changes on this basis today. 
- Follow-up in 6 months, sooner if seizure pattern worsens or daytime convulsions occur. 

Yours sincerely
demos.seizure_frequency_number: 5 cluster per month, multiple per cluster

demos.evidence_text: Device metrics flag one cluster this week; duration three h. He confirms that this pattern has recurred on a weekly basis recently, with multiple brief events within each cluster
demos.note_text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 23 August 2022 

Dr Helen Ward  
Northgate Medical Practice  
14 Northgate, Leeds LS2 8ER  

Dear Dr Helen Ward,  

Michael Turner, DOB: 22-11-1983, Hospital No: L928374 NHS No. 6042197751  
Flat 3, 27 Brookfield Road, Leeds, LS6 4ET 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Recurrent seizures on background of prior stable control; deterioration associated with frequent travel and occasional missed doses; mood low; sleep disruption; vitamin D deficiency under treatment; smoker. 

Medication: Levetiracetam 1500mg twice daily.  
Lamotrigine 100mg twice daily.  
Vitamin D3 800 IU daily.  
Omeprazole 20mg once daily.  

I reviewed Michael Turner in clinic today. He attended alone, arriving directly from a business trip. Over the past eight weeks he has been travelling most weeks, including early flights and irregular mealtimes. He reports increased fatigue, reduced sleep, and acknowledges that he has occasionally missed doses when flights are delayed or luggage is misplaced. He describes feeling more anxious about the unpredictability of events and notes lower mood. 

Seizures: He reports clear worsening compared with earlier this year. Device metrics flag one cluster this week; duration three h. He confirms that this pattern has recurred on a weekly basis recently, with multiple brief events within each cluster, often beginning late evening after days involving travel. He denies prolonged loss of consciousness but describes repetitive episodes of brief unresponsiveness with subsequent confusion, sometimes progressing to whole‑body stiffness. No new injuries, but he has sustained tongue biting twice in the past month. There is no consistent aura. No new triggers other than sleep loss and missed doses. 

Examination: Pulse 74 regular, BP 128/78, weight stable. Neurological examination unremarkable between events; gait normal; no focal deficits elicited today. Skin intact; no head or shoulder bruising. 

Investigations: Home monitor uploads reviewed in clinic corroborate weekly clustering with multiple events per cluster over the last four weeks. Routine bloods three months ago were normal; vitamin D now replete on supplementation. No recent drug interactions identified. 

Assessment: Deteriorating seizure control temporally associated with frequent travel and occasional missed doses. No clear alternative precipitant identified. Risk heightened by weekly clustering and breakthrough events. 

Plan:  
- Emphasised adherence strategies when travelling (carry‑on pill organiser, phone alarms aligned to local time, spare supply in hand luggage).  
- No immediate change to antiepileptic regimen today; will review response to adherence measures within four weeks.  
- Arrange serum levels (lamotrigine trough) and routine bloods in the next 7–10 days.  
- Provide fit note and advised avoiding high‑risk activities until stability returns; discussed DVLA guidance.  
- Book nurse follow‑up in two weeks to review device uploads and adherence log; consultant review in four weeks.  
- Safety: reinforced rescue plan and nocturnal supervision during travel where possible. 

Yours sincerely
demos.seizure_frequency_number: 1 cluster per week, multiple per cluster

fields.description: Seizure frequency in Gan label format. One of: '{count} per {unit}' e.g. '2 per week', '1 per 3 month'; '{count} cluster per {unit}, {n} per cluster'; 'unknown'; 'no seizure frequency reference'; or null to abstain. Unit must be singular: day/week/month/year.
Shortest exact contiguous quote from the note supporting the label. Do not include prompt instructions or wrapper text. Null if abstaining.

signature.instructions:
Extract the Gan seizure-frequency label and supporting evidence from a clinical note.

/no_think
Do not use hidden reasoning. Emit only the requested output fields.

The label must match the Gan annotation vocabulary exactly. Abstain (null) only when
the note contains no usable seizure-frequency information.

Synthesis-backed policy:
- Output only canonical Gan labels: N per unit, N to M per unit, N per M unit,
  N cluster per unit, M per cluster, seizure free for N unit, unknown, or
  no seizure frequency reference.
- Use singular units: day, week, month, year.
- Convert daily/nightly to 1 per day, every N unit to 1 per N unit, and
  numeric "or" ranges to "to" ranges.
- Choose the highest current quantified seizure-type frequency.
- Use the full cluster format; do not drop either the cluster period or
  the per-cluster count.
- Use seizure free for N unit only when seizure freedom is at least 6 months.
  If seizure freedom is shorter than 6 months, compute the rate from the
  total events over the described period.
- For year-to-date counts, use months elapsed since January as the denominator.
- Treat "over the last quarter" as an observation window when the note gives
  a per-month rate; do not automatically convert it to per 3 month.
- evidence_text must be an exact contiguous quote from the note.
